drugs: any drug without clear clinical indication
recommendation: Discontinue any drug without an evidence-based clinical indication
rationale: Frail older adults with limited life expectancy bear medication burdens that often outweigh marginal benefits when no clear indication exists
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021): Lavan AH et al., Age Ageing 50(2):465-471 — Section A1

recommendation: Discontinue drugs the patient is persistently non-adherent to or unable to take correctly
rationale: Persistent non-adherence renders therapy ineffective and increases pill burden without benefit

recommendation: Discontinue drugs causing unacceptable adverse effects affecting quality of life
rationale: In limited life expectancy, quality-of-life harms outweigh marginal mortality benefits

recommendation: Consider discontinuing statins in patients with limited life expectancy (<12 months) for primary or secondary CVD prevention
rationale: Lack of evidence for benefit of statins in primary or secondary prevention in patients with very limited life expectancy; pill burden, cost, myopathy, and side effect risks outweigh benefits. Palliative discontinuation safe per Kutner et al. JAMA Intern Med 2015.
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section B1; Kutner JS et al. JAMA Intern Med 2015;175:691-700

recommendation: Discontinue in advanced frailty with limited life expectancy
rationale: Same rationale as statins; no benefit in this population

recommendation: Discontinue aspirin for primary prevention in patients with limited life expectancy; reassess secondary prevention benefit vs bleeding risk
rationale: No evidence of benefit for primary prevention in very elderly; bleeding risk substantial
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section C1; ASPREE Trial NEJM 2018

recommendation: Reassess risks vs benefits in very frail patients with limited life expectancy; consider discontinuation if bleeding risk exceeds thrombotic risk
rationale: Very frail older adults have high falls risk and thus high intracranial bleeding risk; individualize anticoagulation decisions

recommendation: Discontinue if more than 12 months post-PCI/ACS event in patients with limited life expectancy
rationale: Bleeding risk continues; benefit attenuates beyond 12 months in stable disease

recommendation: Consider dose reduction or discontinuation if SBP consistently <130 mmHg or symptomatic orthostatic hypotension
rationale: Over-treatment of hypertension in frail elderly increases risk of falls, syncope, and acute kidney injury; SPRINT-MIND limits do not extend to advanced frailty

recommendation: Avoid high doses without electrolyte monitoring; consider dose reduction if volume-depleted, hypotensive, or symptomatic dehydration
rationale: Diuretics cause volume depletion, electrolyte imbalances, AKI, and falls in frail elderly

recommendation: Discontinue digoxin if used for rate control of stable AF; reassess if used for systolic HF
rationale: Narrow therapeutic index; renal accumulation; superior alternatives (beta-blockers, ARNi) for HF

recommendation: Discontinue rhythm-control antiarrhythmics in advanced frailty; rate control preferred
rationale: Toxicity risks (pulmonary, hepatic, thyroid for amiodarone) outweigh benefits in limited life expectancy

recommendation: Discontinue in advanced dementia (FAST stage 7) or limited life expectancy
rationale: Marginal benefit on cognition does not extend to severe dementia; GI adverse effects and falls

recommendation: Discontinue in advanced dementia or limited life expectancy
rationale: Marginal cognitive benefit; not sustained in advanced disease

recommendation: Reassess in advanced disease; reduce or discontinue if causing severe psychosis, hallucinations, or impulse control disorders that outweigh motor benefit
rationale: Late-stage PD often features dopaminergic adverse effects exceeding motor benefit

recommendation: Discontinue if no documented evidence of GERD, erosive esophagitis, Barrett's esophagus, peptic ulcer disease, or active NSAID use requiring gastroprotection
rationale: Long-term PPI use without indication causes unnecessary C. diff risk, fracture risk, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, and drug interactions (clopidogrel)

recommendation: Discontinue if no clear ongoing indication
rationale: Often continued without clinical justification; cimetidine has multiple drug interactions

recommendation: Discontinue if eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² or in patients at high risk of lactic acidosis (sepsis, dehydration, hypoxia, decompensated HF)
rationale: Metformin contraindicated in severe renal impairment per FDA labeling due to risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA)
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section H1; FDA Metformin Label 2023

recommendation: Discontinue or substitute with safer agent in advanced frailty due to severe hypoglycemia risk; long-acting agents (glyburide, chlorpropamide) particularly hazardous
rationale: Severe hypoglycemia in frail older adults causes falls, MI, cognitive decline, mortality. Relax glycemic targets (HbA1c 8-9%) in limited life expectancy.
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section H2; ADA Standards of Care 2024 (Older Adults)

recommendation: Simplify insulin regimens in frail elderly; avoid sliding-scale insulin and tight glycemic control (HbA1c <7%)
rationale: Tight glycemic control in frail elderly increases hypoglycemia and mortality without macrovascular benefit (ACCORD-style harm)
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section H3; ACCORD Trial NEJM 2008

recommendation: Use lowest effective dose; target TSH 4-6 mIU/L (not 0.5-2.5) in frail elderly to avoid iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis
rationale: Subclinical thyrotoxicosis in elderly increases AF, osteoporosis, mortality; relaxed TSH target safer
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section H4; ATA Guidelines 2014

recommendation: Reassess in patients with life expectancy <2 years; bisphosphonate fracture-prevention benefit accrues over 12-24 months
rationale: Time-to-benefit for fracture prevention (12-24 months) often exceeds remaining life expectancy in advanced frailty
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section I1; Lee SJ et al. JAMA 2013;310:2609

recommendation: Discontinue or substitute due to anticholinergic burden, orthostatic hypotension, falls, cardiac conduction effects
rationale: Frail older adults have heightened sensitivity to anticholinergic, sedative, and cardiac effects

recommendation: Reassess long-term need; discontinue if used for >12 months without clear ongoing indication and no recent depressive episode
rationale: Hyponatremia (SIADH), bleeding risk, falls; benefits attenuate after sustained remission

recommendation: Taper and discontinue when used for insomnia/anxiety; avoid abrupt discontinuation if chronic use >4 weeks
rationale: Falls, cognitive impairment, dependence; superior non-pharmacologic alternatives

recommendation: Discontinue in patients with dementia-related behavioral symptoms after 12-week trial unless clearly beneficial; address underlying causes (delirium, pain, infection) first
rationale: Increased mortality (FDA black box) and stroke risk in dementia; non-pharmacologic interventions preferred
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section J4; FDA Black Box (Antipsychotics in dementia)

recommendation: Use with extreme caution or discontinue in frail patients with CKD; dose adjustment essential for eGFR <30
rationale: Renally excreted; accumulate in CKD; cause excessive sedation, confusion, falls, and respiratory depression in older adults with impaired renal function

recommendation: Use lowest effective dose; reassess regularly; avoid combination with benzodiazepines or gabapentinoids
rationale: Falls, constipation, respiratory depression; combinations multiply risk
source: STOPPFrail v2 (2021) — Section K2; FDA Drug Safety Comm 2016

recommendation: Discontinue routine nutritional supplements without specific deficiency or indication
rationale: Pill burden without evidence of benefit in non-deficient patients

recommendation: Discontinue; narrow therapeutic index, multiple drug interactions, superior alternatives for COPD
rationale: Toxicity risks (arrhythmia, seizures); inferior to inhaled bronchodilators
